sections.title: How Paper is Made
sections.content_summary: The section introduces paper-making through the story of Pihu, who learns from her mother that paper is made from trees — including drawing books, newspapers, paper napkins, and chart papers. Students learn the steps to make recycled paper at home using old newspapers, fenugreek seeds (methi) as a binding material, and water: soaking, grinding into pulp, rolling into thin sheets, and drying. The section also introduces natural dyes made from plant parts (leaf, flower, root, seed, stem) as safe, traditional colouring agents used on paper, cloth, and pots, and the fact that paper from books is made from wood pulp requiring large numbers of trees, water, and energy. Indian Knowledge System content includes the historical use of cloth, leaves, wooden boards, and palm leaves (talapatra) for writing in India, and banana fibre as an environment-friendly alternative to wood pulp.

tasks.task_text: Make Recycled Paper: Collect materials (old newspapers, fenugreek seeds, water), prepare paper pulp by soaking torn newspaper pieces with fenugreek seeds overnight, blend or mash the soaked mixture, spread the paste on a flat base and roll into thin sheets, then leave to dry.

Colour and design the paper you have made. Natural Colour Experiment: Cut the recycled paper into pieces of same size. Colour them using natural ingredients mentioned in the table below. You can use any other natural ingredients as well. Make sure that the quantity of ingredients used is the same for colouring each piece of paper. Write down your observations in the table below.
Make a greeting card, a paper mask or any such item made of recycled paper.
Do you think that we waste paper?
How can we reduce wastage of paper?

sections.title: Making Responsible Choices
sections.content_summary: This section introduces the 5Rs of waste management — Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repurpose, and Recycle — as a practical framework for reducing waste in daily life. Each R is explained with a concrete example (e.g. refusing single-use paper cups, using both sides of paper, reusing gift bags, repurposing old newspapers into toys, recycling paper). Students explore how small changes help protect the environment, save resources, and reduce pollution. The section also highlights the Braille system as a specialised use of embossed paper enabling visually challenged people to read and write.

tasks.task_text: Discuss the following situations with your classmates and write down which of the 5Rs it belongs to: (table with examples including Maya not using a plastic straw, Rohan using blank pages of old notebooks, Sara giving old books to younger students, Kabir painting an old bottle as a flower vase, Priya cutting old clothes to make a bag, a shopkeeper turning newspapers into paper envelopes).

sections.title: Let Us Get Creative with Paper
sections.content_summary: This section shows students that old and used paper can be transformed into toys and decorative items, illustrating the Repurpose principle in action. Examples include newspaper figures, paper fans, and decorative birds. The section encourages creative thinking about alternative uses for paper as an extension of the 5Rs framework.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: Students reflect on their learning across the chapter by thinking about objects that can be made from paper pulp, applying the 5Rs individually to their own lives, and discussing concrete changes they can make daily to avoid wastage of paper. The reflection consolidates the dual strands of paper-making knowledge and responsible environmental behaviour.

tasks.task_text: What are some objects that you can make using paper pulp? Ask your teachers and elders for more ideas.
Write down one step you can take for each of the 5Rs in the boxes given below.
Discuss the changes you can bring in your daily life by using 5Rs rule to avoid wastage of paper. Complete the table with your ideas for creating these changes.

sections.title: Different Types of Paper and their Uses
sections.content_summary: Students observe and classify different types of paper used in daily life — writing paper, newspaper, art paper, cardboard, and tissue paper — noting that each type has distinct properties suited to its use (tissue is thin, soft, and absorbent; cardboard is thick and hard, used for boxes). The section connects material properties to human purpose and responsible choice-making.

tasks.task_text: Different Types of Paper and their Uses: Observe the different types of paper that we use in our daily life, such as writing paper, newspaper, art paper, cardboard and tissue paper. Paste small pieces of different types of paper in the space given below and write their uses.

dual_strand.natural: Properties of materials and the process of making paper from natural raw materials (wood pulp, plant fibres)
dual_strand.human_cultural: Recycling, responsible use of resources, and the 5Rs of waste management as civic and environmental practice
